_comment: Offline fallback content, authored by Claude to match each generator's schema. Used by fallback_content.py when lm_client.chat() raises LLMUnavailable (LM Studio not connected). It describes ONE coherent settlement -- Emberwell -- so the offline pipeline still reads as a single place, honoring the 'one Settlement, threaded everywhere' rule. theme/biome from the caller override the settlement's theme/biome fields; the authored fiction stays otherwise.

settlement.name: Emberwell
settlement.era: two generations after the Great Burn
settlement.founding_story: Emberwell rose again around a deep cold spring that alone survived the wildfire which swept the valley, drawing back the families who had fled into the hills. Its folk smith iron and burn charcoal under careful watch, and an iron bell hangs at every door to be rung at the first scent of smoke.
settlement.historical_wound: the Great Burn that took the old village
settlement.worldview: the spring remembers what the fire forgets

goal.summary: deepen the cold cistern before the dry season
goal.stakes: if the wells run low again, a single spark could finish what the Great Burn began.

shared_events: the night the bells first rang false
the warden's daughter's wedding by the spring
the season the smiths ran short of ore

subtitles.town: where the anvils never fully cool
subtitles.nature: the spring's green margin, jealously watered
subtitles.ruins: charred timbers the Burn left standing
subtitles.landmark: the cold spring that outlived the fire
subtitles.dungeon: below, where the old pits caved in
subtitles.default: an iron bell waits at every door

relics.name: The Coldspring Draught
relics.description: Water from the spring that would not burn.
relics.lore: Drawn the morning after the Great Burn, it has never once gone warm.

relics.name: Warden Aldra's Warning Horn
relics.lore: Its note still scatters the crows from the charcoal pits at dawn.

relics.name: The Emberwell Charcoal Tally
relics.description: Every cord burned, every bell recast.
relics.lore: Kept since the night the bells first rang false, in case they ever should again.

relics.name: Bell-Iron Nugget
relics.description: Filed from the first bell recast after the Burn.
relics.lore: Carried for luck by every smith who returned to the cold spring.

relics.name: The Unburnt Feather
relics.description: Found whole in the ashes of the old village.
relics.lore: The children say the spring's grey bird dropped it where the fire could not reach.

relics.name: Forge-Master's Ember Pouch
relics.description: Kept to relight the great forge, and never more than that.
relics.lore: In Emberwell even fire is rationed; this pouch is counted twice each night.

relics.name: The Watch-Bell of Emberwell
relics.description: Recast after the old one rang false.
relics.lore: It is struck only when smoke is truly on the wind, so no warden may ever cry wolf again.

relics.name: Ember-Glass Shard
relics.description: Fused in the heat of the Great Burn.
relics.lore: Kept on the council table as proof the whole valley once came within a night of dying.

relics.name: The Smoke-Watch Timepiece
relics.description: Marks the warden's shifts on the wall.
relics.lore: Its slow tick is the only sound on a still night, when every ear is straining for the bells.

relics.name: Aldra's Bucket-Line Token
relics.description: Passed down the line at the first ring of the bells.
relics.lore: Whoever holds it leads the water-chain, and answers for the last bucket.

relics.name: The First Recast Clapper
relics.description: The tongue of the bell that rang false, reforged true.
relics.lore: Kept apart from the new bell, so its old lie can never sound again.

relics.name: Spring-Blessed Copper Cup
relics.description: Dipped once a year in the cold water that would not burn.
relics.lore: The council drinks from it at the founding feast, oldest hand first.

tools.name: Warden Aldra's Trusty Sword
tools.description: The hearth-warden's blade, kept for the long smoke-watches.
tools.lore: Carried on every wall since the night the bells first rang false.

tools.name: Old Pell's Worn Hoe
tools.description: Tends the jealously-watered gardens by the spring.
tools.lore: Pell has turned the green margin with it every dry season since the summer of '31.

tools.name: Bryn's Salvage Axe
tools.description: Pulled from a house the Burn left standing.
tools.lore: Its haft is scorched black but the head still bites clean.

tools.name: The Coldwell Blessing-Blade
tools.description: Drawn only at the spring's yearly blessing.
tools.lore: Gilded by the first smiths who dared return to the cold water.

tools.name: Tomas's Pit Pickaxe
tools.description: For the collapsed charcoal pits below.
tools.lore: The only tool that came back up the season the pit-beams gave way.

tools.name: Smith Hedda's Sturdy Axe
tools.description: Splits cordwood for the charcoal mounds.
tools.lore: Reforged twice for the cistern work, when the smiths ran short of ore.

tools.name: Joss's Patient Rod
tools.description: Worked at the cold spring's outflow at dawn.
tools.lore: Joss fishes the spring-pool so the cistern crews have something hot waiting at dusk.

diaries.page: Third night on the wall, and still I count the bells before I sleep. We must have the cistern deep before the dry months -- Sister Eda says the spring is already low. The night the bells rang false, I learned to trust my own nose over iron.

Turned the green margin again. It drinks more than the spring can spare this year, same as the long dry summer of '31. Aldra wants every bucket for the cistern now. I water at dusk so the warden won't see, and beg the spring's pardon.

Pulled good nails from a house the Burn left standing. Folk say Cinderrow is unlucky, but I married Sael by that same spring and we were glad. The cistern work pays in charcoal, so I dig. An iron bell still hangs in the dead doorway.

The Coldwell has not gone warm in two generations, but it sinks. I keep the tally for Aldra -- so many hands a day on the cistern, so much water held. The spring remembers what the fire forgets, and I mean to help it remember longer.

Back down the charcoal pits since the collapse. We shore the old beams and haul out what ore the smiths ran short of. It is dark work and the air bites, but the cistern needs stone and the forges need fuel. I ring my bell at every shift.

Another day for the cistern. Everyone digs who can. I keep an iron bell by the door as my mother did, and listen for smoke on the wind. We lost a whole village once; we will not lose this cold spring too.

Cold work at the spring-mouth before dawn, but the cistern crews want something hot at dusk. The pool sinks lower each week, same as the long dry summer of '31. Aldra says fish while we still can. I keep my bell on the bank.

Recast a cracked bell today and reforged two hoes for the cistern crews, though we ran short of ore again by dusk. In Emberwell even fire is rationed, and I count my coals twice each night. Every bell I hang is one more nose on the wind against the smoke.

Bryn and I worked the charred timbers again, pulling nails the Great Burn spared. Folk call Cinderrow unlucky, but we were wed by the cold spring and count ourselves blessed. The cistern pays in charcoal, so we dig, and ring the dead doorway's bell coming home.

Took the high watch on Wardens' Rise, where you can see every roof in Emberwell at once. It is a fine, terrible thing, to hold a whole town under your eye and know one spark could take it. I sound the horn only for true smoke, so no warden ever cries wolf again.

Down the old pits with Tomas, shoring beams the collapse left groaning. We haul out what ore we can for Hedda's forge and the cistern's iron. It is dark and the air bites, but the spring remembers what the fire forgets, and I mean to be here to see it filled.

Mother is teaching me the cistern tally -- so many hands a day, so much water held against the dry months. My fingers are stained with ink and ash and I am proud of it. She says a good warden is only counting that never lets its attention wander from the wind.
